name: Iraq War (2003-2011) and insurgency
summary: The Iraq War began with the U.S.-led invasion of March 2003, overthrew the Ba'athist government, and evolved into an occupation, insurgency, sectarian civil conflict, and counterinsurgency until the withdrawal of most U.S. forces in December 2011. A conservative documentary count records about 162,333 direct violent deaths through 2011, while population-based mortality studies estimate several hundred thousand excess deaths, many from indirect war-related causes.

sides.name: U.S.-led coalition and post-2003 Iraqi government/security forces

military_killed.notes: Documented floor: 4,802 U.S./coalition military deaths in 2003-2011 plus Brookings/CRS composite of 10,125 Iraqi police/security-force deaths through July 2011. Iraqi security-force deaths are likely incomplete and not cleanly comparable across sources.

breakdown.cause: air and ground operations during invasion
breakdown.notes: IBC/Guardian reported civilian deaths reaching 7,286 by 1 May 2003, nearly all attributable to U.S.-led coalition forces.
IBC recorded 5,840 civilians killed by airstrikes, including cannon-fire, bombs, and missiles, across 2003-2011.
U.S. Marines killed 24 Iraqi civilians on 19 November 2005.

breakdown.victims_group: Iraqi civilians in Nisour Square, Baghdad
breakdown.notes: Blackwater guards under U.S. contract killed 17 civilians and injured 20 on 16 September 2007.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: IBC's official 2003-2011 assessment gives 15,164 civilians directly caused by the U.S.-led coalition; the Guardian summary of the IBC audit gives 14,705. PLOS/IBC found 11,516 coalition-caused civilian deaths in 2003-2008.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Indirect deaths are not cleanly attributable to one actor. PLOS estimated 405,000 excess Iraqi deaths through June 2011, with more than 60% directly attributable to violence and the rest associated with infrastructure and system collapse; Brown/Costs of War later estimated 250,000 indirect Iraqi civilian deaths from war-related loss of access to food and health care since 2003.

sides.name: Former Ba'athist Iraqi state forces and anti-coalition / anti-government insurgents
members: other anti-coalition and anti-government armed groups
Shia militias when fighting coalition or Iraqi government forces

military_killed.notes: Approximate range: 7,600-10,800 Iraqi combatants killed in the 2003 invasion per Project on Defense Alternatives, plus 23,994 documented post-invasion insurgent/combatant deaths from IBC/Guardian categories. These figures may omit non-battle deaths and may include some misclassified civilians.

breakdown.notes: Lancet/IBC study reported at least 12,000 Iraqi civilians killed by suicide bombings in 2003-2010.

breakdown.victims_group: Yazidi civilians in Qahtaniyah/Til Ezer and Siba Sheikh Khidir
breakdown.notes: Four coordinated suicide vehicle bombs on 14 August 2007 killed 796 people and wounded at least 1,500.

breakdown.victims_group: Iraqi civilians in anti-coalition attacks, short-duration-event subset
breakdown.notes: PLOS/IBC found 3,333 civilian deaths from anti-coalition suicide bombers in the 2003-2008 short-duration-event subset.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: PLOS/IBC attributed 9,954 civilian deaths to anti-coalition forces in 2003-2008. The 2003-2011 high is unknown because many insurgent, militia, sectarian, and criminal attacks were coded as unknown perpetrators.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Insurgent and militia campaigns contributed to displacement, sectarian cleansing, service collapse, and indirect mortality, but the literature does not provide a defensible actor-specific indirect-death total for these groups.

sides.name: Unidentified perpetrators, sectarian death squads, criminal armed groups, and unattributed militia violence

military_killed.notes: No reliable public total separates military/combatant deaths inflicted by unknown or unattributed actors.

breakdown.notes: In the PLOS/IBC short-duration-event subset for 2003-2008, unknown perpetrators executed 19,321 civilians.
Of the execution victims in the PLOS/IBC short-duration-event subset, 5,697 showed signs of torture before execution.
Baghdad had 27,050 civilian deaths in the short-duration-event subset; 11,728 were executions by unknown perpetrators, including 3,863 tortured before execution.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: PLOS/IBC attributed 68,396 of 92,614 documented civilian violent deaths in 2003-2008 to unknown perpetrators, or 74% of the five-year dataset. The full 2003-2011 high is unknown.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Unattributed sectarian and criminal violence contributed to indirect mortality through displacement and insecurity, but no defensible actor-specific estimate exists.

totals.notes: Grand low is the IBC/Guardian documented direct-violent-death roll-up for 2003-2011. Grand high uses the upper bound of the PLOS Medicine 95% uncertainty interval for excess Iraqi deaths through June 2011; it is not a clean sum of side-specific buckets and includes both direct and indirect mortality.

key_atrocities.name: 2003 invasion / Shock and Awe and ground campaign
key_atrocities.notes: IBC/Guardian reported civilian deaths reaching 7,286 by 1 May 2003, nearly all attributable to coalition forces.

key_atrocities.name: Haditha killings
key_atrocities.notes: U.S. Marines killed 24 Iraqi civilians on 19 November 2005.

key_atrocities.name: Sectarian executions and torture killings
key_atrocities.notes: PLOS/IBC found 19,321 civilian executions by unknown perpetrators in the 2003-2008 short-duration-event subset, including 5,697 with signs of torture.

key_atrocities.name: Qahtaniyah / Yazidi bombings
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Insurgent suicide bombers, generally attributed to al-Qaeda in Iraq-linked networks
key_atrocities.notes: Four coordinated suicide vehicle bombs on 14 August 2007 killed 796 people and wounded at least 1,500.

key_atrocities.name: Nisour Square massacre
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Blackwater security contractors under U.S. contract
key_atrocities.notes: Blackwater guards killed 17 civilians and injured 20 in Baghdad on 16 September 2007.

uncertainty_notes: The Iraq War casualty record is highly contested. Documentary counts such as Iraq Body Count provide transparent floors but miss unreported deaths; household surveys estimate excess mortality but have wide uncertainty intervals and methodological controversy. Perpetrator attribution is especially uncertain because most documented civilian deaths in the 2003-2008 PLOS/IBC dataset were attributed to unknown perpetrators. Indirect deaths from infrastructure collapse, displacement, health-system degradation, and insecurity cannot be cleanly assigned to individual belligerents.
